news_summary: Breakthroughs in organ transplantation, memory improvement, inflammation reduction, and pancreatic cancer treatment highlight promising advances in health and medical research.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans les domaines de la transplantation d'organes, de l'amélioration de la mémoire, de la réduction de l'inflammation et du traitement du cancer du pancréas témoignent des progrès prometteurs réalisés dans le domaine de la santé et de la recherche médicale.
news_summary_es: Los avances en el trasplante de órganos, la mejora de la memoria, la reducción de la inflamación y el tratamiento del cáncer de páncreas ponen de relieve los prometedores progresos en el ámbito de la salud y la investigación médica.

articles.title: First pig liver and kidneys transplanted into a person — could ease organ shortages
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 29 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01708-0Organs from genetically modified pigs are being used in trials in China and the US.
articles.content:
Genetically modified pigs, at a farm in Virginia, are being bred for their organs.Credit: Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/AFP via Getty

A 53-year-old clinically dead man has become the first person to receive two kidneys and a whole liver from a genetically modified pig. The man’s organ function was sustained for almost five days with consent from his family, and there were no signs that the organs were being rejected in the first 24 hours, according to a study published in Med today1.

Most procedures in which a pig organ is transplanted into a person — known as xenotransplantation — involve only a single organ. A small number of people have received pig organs, including hearts, kidneys, partial livers and lungs, and clinical trials in living people are under way in the United States and China. Until now, only parts of a pig liver have been transplanted into a person, says Xuyong Sun, a clinician scientist who led the latest procedure at the Second Affiliated Hospital of Guangxi Medical University in Nanning, China.

Transplanting pig kidneys and a liver in the same procedure is also unique, says Leonardo Riella, a physician scientist at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston, who in 2024 led the team that first transplanted a pig kidney into a living person. Transferring multiple organs is more complex than moving one; procedures take longer, increasing the risk of complications, and people who need multiple transplants are often more seriously ill, he adds.

The study shows that multi-organ xenotransplants are possible, says Wayne Hawthorne, a surgeon and transplant researcher at the University of Sydney in Australia.

Multi-organ transplants are already performed with human organs, but there is a shortage of donor organs, which is why research teams are investigating the use of pig kidneys.

Complicated operation

The man who received the pig organs had severe chronic kidney disease and bleeding in his brain before doctors confirmed that his brain had died. His liver was healthy, so it was transplanted into a living person, says Sun.

The man received organs from a pig that had had six edits to its genome. Three human genes were added to reduce the risk of problems with blood clotting, and three pig genes were removed to prevent the organs being rejected.

Within 19 hours of the transplant, the pig liver began secreting bile and showed signs that it was functioning normally, the team reports. The man’s levels of the waste products creatinine and urea — which had been elevated because of his kidney disease — returned to normal after he received the pig kidneys, which suggests they were also functioning.

But 36 hours after the operation, the team noticed early signs that the pig organs were being rejected. For instance, the pig cells in the liver and kidneys were gradually being replaced by human cells, suggesting that the man’s immune system had detected that the organs were foreign. There were also small areas of tissue death and blood clotting in the pig liver.

The authors observed that the transplanted organs had raised levels of a type of immune cell called S100A12+, which is involved in inflammation. They suggest that these cells could be targeted with drugs to reduce the risk of long-term organ rejection.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough in xenotransplantation, where genetically modified pig organs (liver and kidneys) were successfully transplanted into a human for the first time, showing functional organ activity and potential to alleviate the critical shortage of donor organs. The story has broad significance for patients needing transplants worldwide and provides detailed context about the procedure, genetic modifications, and immune response challenges.
articles.personality_title: First successful transplant of pig liver and kidneys into a human shows new hope for organ shortages
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people need organ transplants, but there are not enough human donors. Scientists have been trying to use organs from genetically modified pigs to help solve this problem. This process is called xenotransplantation.

**What happened** – In China, doctors transplanted two kidneys and a whole liver from a genetically modified pig into a 53-year-old man who was clinically dead. The organs worked for almost five days, with no signs of rejection in the first 24 hours. The pig’s genes were changed to make the organs less likely to be rejected by the human body.

**Impact** – This is the first time multiple pig organs—both kidneys and a liver—were transplanted at once into a person. The transplanted organs started working soon after surgery, showing that pig organs can perform important tasks like filtering waste and producing bile. This success suggests pig organs might help reduce the shortage of human donor organs.

**What's next step** – Researchers will study how to prevent the body from rejecting pig organs over longer periods. They plan to develop medicines that target specific immune cells causing inflammation. More clinical trials will test if pig organs can be safely used in living patients needing transplants.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, pig kidneys and a liver were transplanted together into a person, working well and opening new possibilities to address organ shortages worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Première greffe réussie de foie et reins de porc chez un humain ouvre de nouvelles possibilités contre la pénurie d’organes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes ont besoin de greffes d’organes, mais il n’y a pas assez de donneurs humains. Les scientifiques essaient d’utiliser des organes de porcs génétiquement modifiés pour aider à résoudre ce problème. Ce procédé s’appelle la xénogreffe.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En Chine, des médecins ont transplanté deux reins et un foie entier d’un porc génétiquement modifié à un homme de 53 ans cliniquement décédé. Les organes ont fonctionné pendant presque cinq jours, sans signes de rejet durant les premières 24 heures. Les gènes du porc ont été modifiés pour réduire le risque de rejet par le corps humain.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que plusieurs organes de porc — reins et foie — sont transplantés en même temps chez une personne. Les organes greffés ont rapidement commencé à fonctionner, montrant que les organes de porc peuvent accomplir des tâches importantes comme filtrer les déchets et produire de la bile. Ce succès suggère que les organes de porc pourraient aider à réduire la pénurie d’organes humains.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier comment empêcher le corps de rejeter les organes de porc sur de plus longues périodes. Ils veulent développer des médicaments ciblant certaines cellules immunitaires responsables de l’inflammation. D’autres essais cliniques testeront si les organes de porc peuvent être utilisés en toute sécurité chez des patients vivants ayant besoin de greffes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des reins et un foie de porc ont été transplantés ensemble chez une personne, fonctionnant bien et ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour lutter contre la pénurie mondiale d’organes.
articles.personality_title_es: Primera trasplante exitoso de hígado y riñones de cerdo en un humano abre nuevas opciones contra la escasez de órganos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas necesitan trasplantes de órganos, pero no hay suficientes donantes humanos. Los científicos están intentando usar órganos de cerdos genéticamente modificados para ayudar a resolver este problema. Este proceso se llama xenotrasplante.

**Qué pasó** – En China, médicos trasplantaron dos riñones y un hígado completo de un cerdo genéticamente modificado a un hombre de 53 años clínicamente muerto. Los órganos funcionaron casi cinco días, sin señales de rechazo en las primeras 24 horas. Los genes del cerdo fueron modificados para reducir el riesgo de rechazo por el cuerpo humano.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que varios órganos de cerdo — riñones y un hígado — se trasplantan juntos a una persona. Los órganos trasplantados comenzaron a funcionar rápidamente, mostrando que los órganos de cerdo pueden realizar tareas importantes como filtrar desechos y producir bilis. Este éxito sugiere que los órganos de cerdo podrían ayudar a reducir la escasez de órganos humanos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores estudiarán cómo evitar que el cuerpo rechace los órganos de cerdo por más tiempo. Quieren desarrollar medicamentos que ataquen ciertas células del sistema inmune que causan inflamación. Más ensayos clínicos probarán si los órganos de cerdo pueden usarse de forma segura en pacientes vivos que necesitan trasplantes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, riñones y un hígado de cerdo fueron trasplantados juntos en una persona, funcionando bien y abriendo nuevas posibilidades para enfrentar la escasez mundial de órganos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a gentle pig with subtle, glowing circuit-like patterns on its skin, standing beside two intricately intertwined kidneys and a stylized liver made of soft, natural tones, all resting on a smooth, wooden table bathed in soft light symbolizing hope and life.

articles.title: Caffeine reversed memory problems caused by sleep deprivation
articles.summary: Scientists discovered that sleep deprivation damages a key brain circuit responsible for social memory, making it harder to recognize familiar individuals. In laboratory studies, caffeine restored communication between neurons in this pathway and reversed the memory deficits caused by lost sleep. The effect was remarkably targeted, helping the impaired circuit recover without overstimulating normal brain function.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine at the National University of Singapore (NUS Medicine) have found that caffeine can help restore a specific type of memory that is impaired by sleep deprivation. The findings, published in Neuropsychopharmacology, reveal how caffeine acts on a well-defined brain pathway involved in social memory, the ability to recognize and distinguish people we have encountered before.

The research provides new insight into how sleep loss affects the brain and suggests that caffeine's benefits may extend beyond simply increasing alertness.

How Sleep Loss Affects Social Memory

The study was led by Associate Professor Sreedharan Sajikumar and first author Dr. Lik-Wei Wong from the Department of Physiology and the Healthy Longevity Translational Research Program at NUS Medicine.

The team focused on a part of the brain known as the hippocampal CA2 region. The hippocampus is critical for learning and memory, while the CA2 area plays a particularly important role in forming social memories. This brain region also receives signals involved in regulating sleep and wakefulness.

To investigate the effects of sleep deprivation, the researchers subjected laboratory animals to five hours of sleep loss. Afterward, caffeine was provided in drinking water for unrestricted consumption over a seven-day period.

Caffeine Restored Brain Communication

Caffeine is a stimulant that blocks adenosine receptor signaling pathways. Adenosine accumulates during periods of wakefulness and helps reduce brain activity, contributing to feelings of sleepiness.

The researchers then performed electrophysiological recordings on hippocampal tissue samples to assess synaptic plasticity, the brain's ability to strengthen or weaken connections between nerve cells in response to experience and learning.

The results showed that sleep deprivation disrupted the maintenance of synaptic plasticity in the CA2 region. Communication between neurons weakened, reducing the brain's capacity to strengthen important neural connections. These changes were accompanied by noticeable deficits in social recognition memory.

Overall, the findings demonstrated that sleep loss impaired both brain function and behavior through a specific neural circuit.

A Targeted Effect on Memory Circuits

The researchers also found that caffeine administered before sleep deprivation restored synaptic communication in the CA2 region and returned plasticity to normal levels.

As a result, the social memory deficits caused by sleep loss were reversed. Importantly, caffeine's effects were highly selective. Rather than broadly increasing activity throughout the brain, it specifically restored the disrupted pathway linked to social memory.

This targeted action meant that animals in the control group that had not experienced sleep deprivation did not show signs of excessive neural stimulation despite receiving caffeine.

"Sleep deprivation does not just make you tired. It selectively disrupts important memory circuits," noted Dr. Wong. "We found that caffeine can reverse these disruptions at both the molecular and behavioral levels. Its ability to do so suggests that caffeine's benefits may extend beyond simply helping us stay awake."

Assoc Prof Sajikumar added, "Our findings position the CA2 region as a critical hub linking sleep and social memory. This research enhances our understanding towards the biological mechanisms underlying sleep-related cognitive decline. This could inform future approaches to preserving cognitive performance."

Implications for Brain Health and Future Research

The findings highlight the essential role sleep plays in maintaining healthy cognition and memory. By showing that caffeine can restore specific neural pathways affected by sleep deprivation, the study provides new insight into potential targeted approaches for addressing cognitive decline.

The researchers plan to continue investigating how caffeine influences memory consolidation and memory retrieval. Future studies will also use targeted manipulations of brain circuits to better understand the causal relationship between neural pathways and memory function.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery showing that caffeine can reverse memory deficits caused by sleep deprivation by restoring specific brain circuit function. This finding has broad implications for understanding and potentially mitigating cognitive decline related to sleep loss, benefiting general brain health. The study is detailed, focused on a single breakthrough, and highlights a targeted mechanism with potential future applications.
articles.personality_title: Caffeine reverses memory problems caused by lack of sleep
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Sleep deprivation can harm the brain, making it harder to remember people we know. Scientists wanted to see how caffeine affects this problem.

**What happened** – Researchers at the National University of Singapore studied how sleep loss affects a part of the brain called the hippocampal CA2 region, important for social memory. They kept lab animals awake for five hours, then gave them caffeine in their water for a week. They found that caffeine helped restore the brain connections damaged by sleep loss and improved memory.

**Impact** – This is important because caffeine didn’t just make the animals more awake—it fixed the specific brain circuit that sleep deprivation damaged. This shows caffeine can target memory problems caused by lost sleep without overstimulating the whole brain.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to study more about how caffeine helps with memory, including how it supports remembering and learning. They also want to explore brain circuits closely to understand how memory works and how to protect it.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Caffeine can repair the brain’s social memory circuits damaged by sleep loss, improving memory without overstimulating the brain.
articles.personality_title_fr: La caféine inverse les troubles de la mémoire causés par le manque de sommeil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le manque de sommeil peut nuire au cerveau, rendant plus difficile de se souvenir des personnes que l’on connaît. Des scientifiques ont voulu voir comment la caféine agit sur ce problème.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université nationale de Singapour ont étudié l’effet du manque de sommeil sur une partie du cerveau appelée région CA2 de l’hippocampe, importante pour la mémoire sociale. Ils ont privé des animaux de sommeil pendant cinq heures, puis leur ont donné de la caféine dans l’eau pendant une semaine. Ils ont découvert que la caféine aidait à restaurer les connexions cérébrales endommagées par le manque de sommeil et améliorait la mémoire.

**Impact** – C’est important car la caféine n’a pas seulement rendu les animaux plus éveillés, elle a réparé le circuit cérébral spécifique abîmé par le manque de sommeil. Cela montre que la caféine peut cibler les problèmes de mémoire liés au sommeil sans surstimuler tout le cerveau.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent étudier davantage comment la caféine aide la mémoire, notamment comment elle soutient le souvenir et l’apprentissage. Ils souhaitent aussi mieux comprendre les circuits cérébraux pour protéger la mémoire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La caféine peut réparer les circuits cérébraux de la mémoire sociale endommagés par le manque de sommeil, améliorant la mémoire sans surstimuler le cerveau.
articles.personality_title_es: La cafeína revierte problemas de memoria causados por falta de sueño
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La falta de sueño puede dañar el cerebro, dificultando recordar a personas conocidas. Científicos quisieron investigar cómo la cafeína afecta este problema.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Nacional de Singapur estudiaron cómo la pérdida de sueño afecta una parte del cerebro llamada región CA2 del hipocampo, importante para la memoria social. Privaron a animales de sueño por cinco horas y luego les dieron cafeína en el agua durante una semana. Descubrieron que la cafeína ayudó a restaurar las conexiones cerebrales dañadas y mejoró la memoria.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la cafeína no solo hizo que los animales estuvieran más despiertos, sino que reparó el circuito cerebral específico dañado por la falta de sueño. Esto muestra que la cafeína puede tratar problemas de memoria causados por la pérdida de sueño sin sobreestimular todo el cerebro.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar más cómo la cafeína ayuda con la memoria, incluyendo cómo apoya el recordar y el aprendizaje. También quieren explorar los circuitos cerebrales para entender mejor la memoria y cómo protegerla.

**Resumen en una frase** – La cafeína puede reparar los circuitos cerebrales de la memoria social dañados por la falta de sueño, mejorando la memoria sin sobreestimular el cerebro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing hippocampus-shaped tree with intertwined neural branches forming a delicate network, where vibrant coffee beans hang like fruit restoring bright, sparkling connections between the branches, set against a soft, muted background symbolizing restful night turning into alert morning.

articles.title: This tomato-soy juice reduced inflammation in just four weeks
articles.summary: A specially formulated tomato-soy juice packed with natural plant compounds may help calm inflammation linked to obesity, according to a new clinical study. Healthy adults with obesity who drank the juice daily for four weeks saw significant reductions in several key inflammatory proteins in their blood, while a control tomato juice did not produce the same effect.
articles.content:
Drinking a specially formulated tomato-soy juice rich in plant compounds linked to health benefits reduced several markers of inflammation in adults with obesity after just four weeks, according to new research.

The findings suggest the beverage could serve as a functional food capable of helping control chronic inflammation, a process that contributes to many long-term health conditions.

Researchers found that the juice, which contains high levels of lycopene and soy isoflavones, significantly lowered blood concentrations of three proteins associated with systemic inflammation when compared with a control tomato juice that lacked those compounds.

"The idea is, can we use food-based interventions to modulate inflammation?" said lead author Jessica Cooperstone, associate professor of horticulture and crop science at The Ohio State University. "And can we test this in a rigorous way so that we can really see this is affecting inflammation, versus just saying something is anti-inflammatory?"

Building on these results and other supporting evidence, Cooperstone and colleagues have secured funding from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases for a pilot clinical trial investigating whether the same tomato-soy juice can reduce inflammation in people with pancreatitis.

The study was recently published in the journal Molecular Nutrition & Food Research.

Why Tomatoes and Soy?

Lycopene is a carotenoid responsible for the red color of tomatoes and certain other fruits and vegetables. Soy isoflavones belong to a group of compounds called flavonoids and can mimic some actions of the hormone estrogen. Both are naturally occurring phytochemicals that play important roles in plant health.

The tomato-soy juice used in the study was developed years ago by Ohio State researchers after earlier studies suggested that diets rich in tomato products or soy were associated with a lower risk of prostate cancer. The juice was made using tomatoes specially bred to contain elevated levels of lycopene and was fortified with soy isoflavone extract.

Subsequent research at Ohio State found that increased consumption of the juice was linked to lower prostate-specific antigen levels in some men with prostate cancer. Other studies have also indicated that tomatoes and soy, either separately or together, may influence inflammatory and metabolic processes connected to obesity and other chronic diseases.

"There's been enough compelling evidence that compounds from tomatoes and soy might be modulating inflammation that we decided to test this in people," Cooperstone said.

Human Trial Reveals Reduced Inflammation

The study involved 12 healthy adults with obesity. Participants drank two 6-ounce cans of the tomato-soy juice each day for four weeks. Following a washout period, they consumed a low-carotenoid tomato juice for another four weeks.

"The hypothesis is that it's the lycopene from the tomatoes and the isoflavones from the soy that's inducing the effect, so we didn't want to have a control that's just water," Cooperstone said.

Researchers collected blood samples before and after each four-week period and measured cytokines, which are inflammatory proteins produced by the immune system.

Only the tomato-soy juice produced significant reductions in three cytokines: Interleukin (IL)-5, IL-12p70 and granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF). Researchers also observed a decrease in tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-a), though that change did not reach statistical significance.

Changes Seen Beyond Inflammatory Markers

The team also examined participants' urine samples before and after each phase of the study to look for shifts in metabolites, the molecules produced when the body breaks down nutrients and carries out essential biological processes.

Some metabolite changes appeared after consumption of both the tomato-soy juice and the control tomato juice, indicating that tomatoes may produce biological effects even without high levels of lycopene.

However, changes related to soy isoflavone metabolites stood out in participants who consumed the tomato-soy juice. While researchers say more study is needed, these results provide additional evidence that the beverage is influencing human biology in measurable ways.

"This is probably a function of the fact that there's more to our intervention agents than just these two compounds," Cooperstone said. "Ultimately, we want to have a better understanding of how the foods that we eat are relating to our health. And when we really want to be sure, we need to test them in clinical trials. And that's what we're doing here."

Potential Benefits for Pancreatitis Patients

The research team has also gathered evidence from animal studies suggesting that the tomato-soy juice can lessen inflammation and reduce the severity of chronic pancreatitis.

Those findings helped support the launch of the new clinical trial focused on pancreatitis patients. Researchers hope the beverage could offer benefits for a condition that currently has limited treatment options.

"Care for patients with pancreatitis is palliative, focused on controlling pain and GI symptoms. Our hypothesis is that the tomato-soy juice may serve as an intervention to decrease inflammation and hopefully increase patients' quality of life," Cooperstone said.

The work was supported by the U.S. Department of Agriculture, the National Institutes of Health, the Lisa and Dan Wampler Endowed Fellowship for Foods and Health Research, and the Foods for Health Initiative at Ohio State.

Co-authors included first author Maria Sholola, Jenna Miller, Emma Bilbrey, David Francis and Thomas Mace of Ohio State, along with Janet Navotny of the USDA. Mace serves as the lead principal investigator of the pancreatitis trial. Cooperstone, Philip Hart and Kristen Roberts of Ohio State are also principal investigators on the study.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a clinical study demonstrating that a specially formulated tomato-soy juice significantly reduces inflammation markers in adults with obesity, indicating a potential functional food intervention to control chronic inflammation linked to many diseases. The study has broad health implications, is supported by rigorous research, and is advancing to clinical trials for pancreatitis patients, addressing a condition with limited treatment options.
articles.personality_title: Tomato-soy juice lowers inflammation in just four weeks, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Chronic inflammation is linked to many health problems like obesity and pancreatitis. Researchers are exploring if certain foods can help reduce this inflammation.

**What happened** – Scientists at Ohio State University tested a special tomato-soy juice on 12 adults with obesity. The juice contains lycopene from tomatoes and isoflavones from soy, both natural plant compounds. Participants drank two cans daily for four weeks. Blood tests showed significant drops in three proteins related to inflammation. A control tomato juice without soy did not have the same effect.

**Impact** – This is one of the first studies to prove with real evidence that a tomato-soy drink can lower inflammation markers in humans. It shows that food can play a role in controlling inflammation, which may help prevent or manage diseases linked to obesity.

**What’s next step** – Researchers have started a new clinical trial to see if the juice can reduce inflammation in people with pancreatitis, a painful condition with few treatment options. This trial will test if the juice improves patients’ quality of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Drinking a tomato-soy juice daily reduced key inflammation proteins in adults with obesity, opening new possibilities for food-based health interventions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un jus de tomate et soja réduit l'inflammation en seulement quatre semaines, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'inflammation chronique est liée à de nombreux problèmes de santé comme l'obésité et la pancréatite. Des chercheurs étudient si certains aliments peuvent aider à réduire cette inflammation.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques de l'Université d'État de l'Ohio ont testé un jus spécial à base de tomate et de soja sur 12 adultes obèses. Le jus contient du lycopène des tomates et des isoflavones du soja, deux composés naturels. Les participants ont bu deux canettes par jour pendant quatre semaines. Des analyses sanguines ont montré une baisse importante de trois protéines liées à l'inflammation. Un jus de tomate sans soja n'a pas eu le même effet.

**Impact** – C'est l'une des premières études à prouver avec des preuves solides qu'un jus tomate-soja peut diminuer les marqueurs d'inflammation chez l'humain. Cela montre que l'alimentation peut jouer un rôle dans le contrôle de l'inflammation, ce qui pourrait aider à prévenir ou gérer des maladies liées à l'obésité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs ont lancé un nouvel essai clinique pour voir si ce jus peut réduire l'inflammation chez des personnes atteintes de pancréatite, une maladie douloureuse avec peu d'options de traitement. Cet essai testera si le jus améliore la qualité de vie des patients.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Boire quotidiennement un jus tomate-soja réduit des protéines clés de l'inflammation chez les adultes obèses, ouvrant de nouvelles pistes pour des interventions santé basées sur l'alimentation.
articles.personality_title_es: Un jugo de tomate y soja reduce la inflamación en solo cuatro semanas, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inflamación crónica está vinculada a muchos problemas de salud como la obesidad y la pancreatitis. Los investigadores buscan si ciertos alimentos pueden ayudar a reducir esta inflamación.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Estatal de Ohio probaron un jugo especial de tomate y soja en 12 adultos con obesidad. El jugo contiene licopeno de tomate e isoflavonas de soja, compuestos naturales. Los participantes bebieron dos latas al día durante cuatro semanas. Los análisis de sangre mostraron una reducción significativa en tres proteínas relacionadas con la inflamación. Un jugo de tomate sin soja no produjo el mismo efecto.

**Impacto** – Este es uno de los primeros estudios que demuestra con evidencia real que un jugo de tomate y soja puede bajar marcadores de inflamación en humanos. Muestra que la alimentación puede ayudar a controlar la inflamación, lo que podría prevenir o manejar enfermedades relacionadas con la obesidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores iniciaron un nuevo ensayo clínico para ver si el jugo puede reducir la inflamación en personas con pancreatitis, una enfermedad dolorosa con pocas opciones de tratamiento. Este estudio probará si el jugo mejora la calidad de vida de los pacientes.

**Frase clave** – Beber diariamente un jugo de tomate y soja redujo proteínas clave de inflamación en adultos con obesidad, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para intervenciones de salud basadas en alimentos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant, glowing glass of rich red tomato-soy juice surrounded by stylized floating molecular structures representing lycopene and soy isoflavones, with softly glowing abstract representations of reduced inflammation—such as calming, smooth waves or gentle blue and green hues—radiating outward, all rendered in natural, simple colors against a soft, neutral background.

articles.title: Pancreatic cancer halted by virus injection in three patients
articles.summary: A cancer-killing virus has stopped pancreatic tumours from growing and spreading in three people in an initial safety trial, raising hopes that it may help to beat the deadly condition
articles.content:
A cancer-killing virus has stopped pancreatic tumours from growing and spreading in three people in an initial safety trial, raising hopes that it may help to beat the deadly condition

A scanning electron micrograph of pancreatic cancer cells ANNE WESTON, EM STP, THE FRANCIS CRICK INSTITUTE/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

A virus has stopped pancreatic cancer in its tracks in three people in a clinical trial in the US. Further evaluation is needed in larger trials, but the early results are encouraging, especially since only small doses of the virus were administered for initial safety testing.

“We only injected one-tenth of the dose we are eventually aiming at, so the efficacy is better than I expected, especially as this is pancreatic cancer,” says Masato Yamamoto at the University of Minnesota, who led the development of the viral treatment.

Pancreatic cancer is notorious for being the deadliest type of the condition. One reason is that symptoms tend to appear late, when the cancer has already spread and cannot be surgically removed. Once diagnosed, people usually live only around three to six months.

Another reason is that pancreatic tumours have tough, fibrous interiors that block chemotherapy drugs from getting in. “They’re hard as hockey pucks,” says Yamamoto. Immunotherapies that boost immune activity against cancer are also ineffective, because pancreatic tumours can hide from the immune system.

The first patient in the trial, who had a pancreatic tumour 7 centimetres across, was administered the treatment a year ago, and the other two have been since then. At the time, their tumours hadn’t spread beyond the pancreas. Since being treated, their tumours haven’t grown any further. “They are all still alive and have clinically stable disease,” says Yamamoto, who presented the results at the annual meeting of the American Society of Gene and Cell Therapy in Boston, Massachusetts, earlier this month. Another 15 patients will now be given higher doses to find the optimum level.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

“I think this is an interesting early signal, but as a pancreatic surgeon, I think it’s important to keep perspective,” says Kai Brown at Royal North Shore Hospital in Sydney. “The history of oncology is littered with promising early signals that vanished by the time rigorous phase III [late-stage] testing was done, so I think these preliminary conference results should probably just be seen as hypothesis-generating at this stage.” He also notes that the trial so far hasn’t included a control group, so it is hard to know whether the cancer-killing virus works better than other treatments or none at all.

The virus is an adenovirus that has been genetically engineered to replicate only inside tumours and not healthy tissue. Its replication is specifically activated by an enzyme called cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), which is present at much higher levels in cancer cells than in normal ones. After cancer cells become infected with the virus, they burst open and die, releasing more virus that can infect neighbouring cancer cells.

In the trial, the virus was injected directly into the patients’ tumours through a thin tube that was guided down a patient’s throat to their pancreas. The tube had an ultrasound probe on the end to allow visualisation of the tumours.

The reason the tumours have stopped growing but haven’t become any smaller may be due to the low treatment dose, says Yamamoto. He thinks they may start to shrink as the virus has more time to replicate.

As the tumour cells start to break apart and leak their contents, the immune system may also be able to recognise and fight the cancer, says Yamamoto. “The patient’s immune system can realise something is wrong, and then go and attack the tumour,” he says. The hope is that the immune system will also recognise and destroy any tumour cells that have spread to other parts of the body. If so, the treatment could be effective against metastatic pancreatic cancer.

To try to supercharge this natural immune response, Yamamoto and his colleagues are now planning to combine the viral treatment with immunotherapies like checkpoint inhibitors – drugs that block proteins that stop the immune system from attacking the cancer cells – in future clinical trials.

Adenoviruses, which cause cold- and flu-like symptoms in their unmodified forms, have a long history of being investigated as potential cancer treatments. In the 1950s, for instance, women with cervical cancer were injected with an unmodified adenovirus in a clinical trial, with partial success. However, it became clear that adenoviruses must be engineered to make them selectively target cancer cells, for safety and efficacy reasons.

The only cancer-killing virus that has been approved by the Food and Drug Administration in the US is T-VEC, a genetically modified herpes simplex virus that is injected directly into melanoma tumours, causing the cells to rupture and die.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a promising medical breakthrough where a genetically engineered virus halted pancreatic tumor growth in three patients during an initial clinical trial. Pancreatic cancer is highly lethal with few effective treatments, so this advancement offers hope for a deadly disease affecting many people. The article provides detailed context about the trial, mechanism, and future plans, indicating significant potential impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: Virus injection stops pancreatic tumor growth in early trial
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Pancreatic cancer is one of the deadliest cancers because it is usually found late and is hard to treat. Its tumors are tough and block medicines, making it difficult for chemotherapy and some immune treatments to work.

**What happened** – In a new clinical trial in the US, a specially designed virus was injected directly into the pancreatic tumors of three patients. This virus only attacks cancer cells and makes them burst open, stopping the tumors from growing. The treatment used a small dose, and all three patients’ tumors stayed the same size and did not spread for many months.

**Impact** – This is an important early result because pancreatic cancer usually grows quickly and is hard to control. The virus works in a new way by targeting cancer cells without harming healthy ones. It also might help the body’s immune system to fight the cancer better.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will give higher doses of the virus to 15 more patients to find the best amount to use. They also plan to combine the virus with other immune-boosting drugs to try to make the treatment even stronger. Larger and longer studies will be needed to confirm if this approach really works well.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new virus treatment has safely stopped pancreatic tumors from growing in three patients, opening the door for better therapies against this tough cancer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une injection virale arrête la croissance des tumeurs du pancréas lors d’un essai initial
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer du pancréas est l’un des plus mortels car il est souvent détecté tard et difficile à traiter. Ses tumeurs sont dures et bloquent les médicaments, ce qui rend la chimiothérapie et certains traitements immunitaires inefficaces.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors d’un nouvel essai clinique aux États-Unis, un virus spécialement conçu a été injecté directement dans les tumeurs pancréatiques de trois patients. Ce virus attaque uniquement les cellules cancéreuses et les fait éclater, arrêtant ainsi la croissance des tumeurs. Le traitement a utilisé une faible dose, et les tumeurs des trois patients sont restées stables et n’ont pas progressé pendant plusieurs mois.

**Impact** – Ce résultat préliminaire est important car le cancer du pancréas se développe généralement rapidement et est difficile à contrôler. Le virus agit d’une nouvelle manière en ciblant les cellules cancéreuses sans endommager les cellules saines. Il pourrait aussi aider le système immunitaire à mieux combattre le cancer.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont administrer des doses plus élevées du virus à 15 autres patients pour trouver la dose optimale. Ils prévoient aussi de combiner ce virus avec d’autres médicaments qui stimulent le système immunitaire pour renforcer le traitement. Des études plus larges et plus longues seront nécessaires pour confirmer l’efficacité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau traitement viral a stoppé en toute sécurité la croissance des tumeurs pancréatiques chez trois patients, ouvrant la voie à de meilleures thérapies contre ce cancer difficile.
articles.personality_title_es: Una inyección viral detiene el crecimiento de tumores pancreáticos en ensayo inicial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer de páncreas es uno de los más mortales porque suele detectarse tarde y es difícil de tratar. Sus tumores son duros y bloquean los medicamentos, dificultando que la quimioterapia y algunos tratamientos inmunitarios funcionen.

**Qué pasó** – En un nuevo ensayo clínico en Estados Unidos, un virus especialmente diseñado fue inyectado directamente en los tumores pancreáticos de tres pacientes. Este virus solo ataca las células cancerosas y las hace estallar, deteniendo el crecimiento de los tumores. El tratamiento usó una dosis pequeña, y los tumores de los tres pacientes se mantuvieron del mismo tamaño y no se expandieron durante varios meses.

**Impacto** – Este resultado inicial es importante porque el cáncer de páncreas suele crecer rápido y es difícil de controlar. El virus funciona de una manera nueva al atacar solo las células cancerosas sin dañar las sanas. También podría ayudar al sistema inmunitario a combatir mejor el cáncer.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores administrarán dosis mayores del virus a 15 pacientes más para encontrar la cantidad ideal. También planean combinar el virus con otros medicamentos que potencien el sistema inmunitario para hacer el tratamiento más fuerte. Se necesitarán estudios más grandes y prolongados para confirmar si esta terapia funciona bien.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo tratamiento viral detuvo con seguridad el crecimiento de tumores pancreáticos en tres pacientes, abriendo la puerta a mejores terapias contra este cáncer difícil.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, intricately patterned virus gently enveloping and breaking apart a textured, fibrous pancreatic tumor represented as a dense, puck-like mass, surrounded by softly illuminated, abstract immune cells awakening and moving toward the tumor, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.
